history:
Teacher: This morning, we are going to talk about circles.  What’s a circle?
Student: A circle is a shape.
Student: It’s a shape.
Teacher: It’s a shape.  And what are some of the things that you know are made that look like circles?  Student E?
Student: Like a ball?
Teacher: A ball; that’s a good one.  Student J.
Student: A pie.
Teacher: A pie; mm, food.  Student M.
Student: The can.
Teacher: The can, okay.  Student R.
Student: A clock.
Teacher: Clock.  Student S.
Student: An orange.
Teacher: An orange.  Student A.
Student: An apple.
Teacher: Is that a circle?  Have you ever seen a circle that is shaped – I mean an apple that is shaped like this?  Student K?
Student: A bowl.
Teacher: A bowl?  Mm, yeah; that’s a good one.  Student G?
Student: The spoon part, the end of the spoon.
Teacher: That looks like a circle.  This is an example of a circle. Have you ever seen a spoon shaped like this?
Student: This?  Yes.
Teacher: Completely like a circle.  Student B.
Student: The table.  A table.
Teacher: Tables.  Okay, that’s a good one too.  Who haven’t I heard from?  Student A.
Student: A donut.
Teacher: Food again!  A donut.  Okay, alright.  Student T?  Any ideas on circles?  Something that’s shaped like a circle.
Student: A cake.
Teacher: A cake.
Student: Some cakes are circles.
Teacher: See, now we sure got cakes going on.  We had donuts.  We had pies.  See, you know, we could end the lesson on food.  But we can’t do that today.  We have to keep going.  Good answers everybody. Our standard says: to identify, measure and describe circles and the relationships of the radius, diameter, circumference and chord.  These are four things that can be located in the use of a circle, right: radius, diameter, circumference and chord.  We’re gonna talk about these today.  You ever heard of these words before?
Multiple students: Yes.
Teacher: You’ve heard of these words before?  Good.  Okay, that makes it a little bit easier for us.  Does anybody remember what radius is?  Do you remember what radius is?  No?  Yes? Okay.  We’re going to talk about circles to the point that you’re going to create your own and, in the creation of your own, you are going to label it.  Alright?  So I need you to really, really be focused. If you would please, take a plate and pass it.  Alright?  Everybody has on their desk a larger sheet of white paper, correct?
Multiple students: Yes.
Teacher: Make sure – let me have one of those please.
Student: Okay.
Teacher: Thank you; okay; alright.  Just as in many geometric shapes, circles have parts.  You have parts don’t you?
Multiple students: Yes.
Teacher: How many parts do you have on your body?  The ones that we can see starting with your –
Multiple students: Head.
Teacher: Head; okay.  And from your shoulders to your waist, that’s called a torso.
Student: Torso.
Teacher: Right?  And then, from the waist down to –
Student: Your legs.
Teacher: They have legs.  What’s attached to the legs?
Multiple students: Feet.
Teacher: Feet.  What’s attached to the shoulders?
Multiple students: Arms.
Teacher: And what’s attached to the arms?
Multiple students: Hands.
Teacher: And those are parts, correct?
Multiple students: Yes.
Teacher: Circles have parts too.  Thank you.  Circles have parts too. And today we are going to identify those parts.  And I want you to also know their function.  If you would, take your – I’ve provided everybody a plate.  Take your plates; you’re gonna have to turn it facedown.  Turn it face down on your paper.  And I want to identify the parts of the circle with the particular color on purpose.  To get us started, use your pencil if you would, please sir, please ma’am; kind of center your plate in the middle and I want you to draw a circle.  You’re going to trace the plate and draw a circle. And we’re not looking for perfection.  We’re just looking for circles. Alright.  Try to do it dark enough so you can see it without a problem.  In the middle of a circle, we have a center point.  I want to use this just to make sure.  I’ve drawn a circle up here on the board just as you did.  If you would, as best you can, I want you to put a dot in the middle.  I want to see something if at all possible; measuring on purpose here.  Excuse me. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 – almost at 9; just about at 9, so a 4 and a half.  Do you have your rulers on your desk?
Multiple students: Yes.
Teacher: If you have it on your desk, you’re getting ready to use it. Take your ruler if you would and you’re going to lay the ruler across the circle that you just drew; plastic one; the plastic one.  I don't know.  Maybe the hard wood one would work.  What you’re trying to do, I just measured Student R’s and, going all the way across the plate, if you measure it kind of in the middle, it will come up to measure at 9 inches going across.  Do you see that?  It’s close to 9 inches.  Do you see that?
Student: Yes.
Teacher: Yes or no?
Student: Yes.
Student: No.
Teacher: Try it; close to 9 inches going across.  Use this one for now. Close to 9 inches.  Well what’s half of 9 or half 9 is? About 4 and a half, okay?  So, at 4 and a half on your ruler, which is the long line between the 4 and the 5; that’s the midway point.  If you’re close to that, then you measure with your eyes and you did a fantastic job.  That’s where your dot should be.  That’s where your center point should be, okay?  If you have it at 4 and a half, fine. That’s pretty close.  You can move it over.  You can put your dot there and erase the other one.  Do it!  You have yours – make sure that the line here on the ruler lines up with the circle right here.  Right there.  That’s your 4 and a half. Wake up.  Come on!  Come on; 4 and a half; 4 and a half.  Do you see where 4 and a half is?
Student: Yeah.
Teacher: Right here; good, good, good; 4 and a half.  You got it?  These are some of the things that architects and engineers have to know how to do when they’re designing buildings and things and structures.  They have to make sure that their measurements are as close to accurate as possible.  Okay. Everybody has a dot there.
Multiple students: Yes.
Teacher: Well, for math, we’re gonna call it our center point because the center point helps us to do a lot of things when it comes to the circle.  So that’s why you need to have your center point.  Okay?  Center point is not a part of this, but – within the standard – but it is very helpful when it comes to the measurement.  So everybody has their center point.
Multiple students: Yes.
Teacher: Now, what I see on some, which I really like, you really made it dark.  You also kind of made it not huge, but you made it big enough so that it is not a problem to be able to see.  And that’s a good thing. First thing we’re gonna talk about is radius.  Radius has to do with the distance from the center of the circle to the edge of the circle.  So, with the ruler that you are working with, with the ruler, if you would please, sir, please ma’am, use your ruler – and I’m gonna borrow this one real quick – to draw a line from my center point to the – let me hold this still – to the edge of the circle and that way you’ll see what I’m talking about. Okay?  Do that!  It is the distance from the center to a point on the circle; from the center to a point.  And it doesn’t necessarily have to be here.  I could have drawn it here or here or here or here.  But I decided to just draw it here. But this is known as radius.  Okay? After you draw it, would you please identify the line?  Identify that line as radius.  And so it’s even more identifiable, would you go back and trace that line – use your ruler.  And you don’t have to but, if you don’t have a steady hand, would you also trace it in red.  Okay?  Trace that line in red. What do we call this line?
Multiple students: Radius.
Teacher: We call this line radius.  Why is it known as radius?
Student: Because it’s on –
Teacher: Tell me again.  Give me a rough definition as to what radius is.  Student S?
Student: Radius is a line that can be drawn across a circle.
student: Radius is a line that can be drawn across a circle.
human: Where do you start?
llm: Close, but not all the way across. Look at the red line we just drew. Where did we start? We started right at the center point and drew the line just to the edge. So the radius is the distance from the center point to the edge of the circle.

Teacher: There are some cubes on my table, so take the ones that are in sets already.
Student: [inaudible]
Teacher: Say that again?  You can do it that way, that's fine.  I know there's not a lot of room there.
Teacher: Okay, another thing that - we're missing two equations for each one.  You should have two equations for each one.  You know what?  Student A, I'll have to write down what I see going on here and it's not all good.  I'd like to write down that I see you working hard.
Student: I am working hard.
Teacher: So are you helping him?  Did you notice he's doing it wrong? If he were my partner, I'd be telling him that.  Tell him. What?
Student: [inaudible]
Teacher: So I'm gonna watch to see how you're gonna recover right now, and do the right thing, both of you.  Wow, some [inaudible].  You know what?  I'm gonna wait, but I would really like to see that you know how to work with a partner.
Student: I have one.
Teacher: Student A, you're gonna talk to him.  Everybody, S-T-O-P stop. You know what?  I said stop.
Teacher: Student J, take that hat off right now.  I see very few groups working together.  So we are going to stop because this is a waste of time unless we can work together. I want to see that you have your two sheets, and that you're not being careless.  You're checking the floor around you. You're checking for pencils and books.  G-O go.
Teacher: Now, I said quietly, Student J.  I'm gonna try that again.  I said - Student A! I should see you cleaning up.  Student D. Student J, do I see you cleaning up?  Then go help clean up the cubes on the back table, organize the papers that are left out, please.  I love how this group's been working so nicely together.  The four of you have done an excellent job.  Please make sure your names are on your paper. Student I, I'm proud of you today.  I like how Student G and Student O were working hard together, nice job. Synergizing and discussing the math.  Clean this up, please.
Teacher: Excuse me.  Boys in the library, out of there, please.  You know what, Student V?  Instead of stepping on that book, please pick it up.
Teacher: Yeah, hold onto it.  You hold onto Student P's stuff.  You were his partner.  You know what?  Those people who were at the Tigers table, I see a chair sack on the ground.  I see a lot of chairs out of place.
Teacher: All right.  I'm just very curious if there is a partnership who really thinks they were synergizing.  Okay.  Why do you think you were synergizing, Student O?  What were you two saying to each other?
Student: She wanted to do 7 times 12 and I wanted to do 7 times 11.  But I told her I would like to do my high numbers [inaudible]. She decided to do 7 times 11.
Teacher: So you were making compromises in how you were solving it?  Did each of you have to solve it the same way?
Multiple students: No.
Teacher: No, so that's nice that she did that.  But Student O, if Student G wants to use her own strategy, she could do it, and if you want to use your own, that's okay.  But what I saw these girls doing here is they were talking to each other about the math.
Teacher: That they were working together, they were reading the problem together and that's what a good synergizing partnership would look like.  Who thinks they could model for us, with their partner, how to play missing factors - you know what I'm gonna write, Student G?  Who thinks they can model for the entire class how to play this game correctly?  I would not pick you guys 'cause I'm not sure that you were all synergizing.  So I would agree - no, I'm sorry.  When I looked over, I did not see synergy at that table.  Student C and Student T, I saw synergy.  Could you two switch seats with Student J and Student A?  Maybe Student J could sit up there.  Student J, could you bring the array cards over, please?  Student J, go sit on the bench, please.  You have some.
Teacher: I want you paying attention.  What do you notice as they play? So I'll know you're paying attention when your eyes are on Student T and Student C, like Student J's.  Like Student J is looking.  Student A, stand up if you have to.  I like how Student I is standing up.
Student: [inaudible]
Teacher: You boys don't need to see.  You can listen.
Student: [inaudible]
Student: I know that 72 divided by 9 equals 8.
Teacher: Student C, do you agree?
Student: Yes, I agree.
Teacher: Okay, S-T-O-P stop.  Were they yelling?
Multiple students: No.
Teacher: Were they addressing each other?
Multiple students: Yeah.
Teacher: Were they talking to each other?
Multiple students: No
Teacher: Well, could Student T play this game without Student C?
Multiple students: No.
Teacher: Why?  What was she doing with Student C, and what was Student C using Student T for?  I mean, if I were playing this game by myself, I probably would play it very differently than the two of them were just playing.  Student J, what did you notice?
Student: Student C was correcting Student T if the factors were right.
student: Student C was correcting Student T if the factors were right.
human: How did she know what she was choosing?  What did Student T do to let Student C know which card she was choosing?
llm: Exactly! Student C was checking Student T's work to make sure the missing factor was correct. That is exactly what it means to be a good partner and synergize. You are there to help each other, check each other's math, and talk about your equations using your indoor voices. You can't just play by yourself; you have to communicate. Now, I want to see everyone using this exact same teamwork. Let's head back to our centers and try this again quietly.

Student: Look at the fractions.
Teacher: Look at the fractions.  And we’ve got to make sure that the fractions have the same bottom number or we can’t add them. So what do we do, using math vocabulary, what do we do to make it possible to add, Student T?
Student: First look at the denominator, and then you look at both the numbers to see what number you can use to make them the same.
student: First look at the denominator, and then you look at both the numbers to see what number you can use to make them the same.
human: What’s another word for that?  We’re making a…

student: Another one, I thought we’ve got to see if one can be a multiple of the other.
human: I like that, that’s exactly right.  To save yourself some trouble you want to make sure you look at those. student: When we’re subtracting and we have different denominators, do we still multiply?
human: But are those multiples of each other though?
llm: Yes, exactly. You still have to multiply to find a common denominator and make equivalent fractions, just like you did for adding. The only difference is that once your denominators are the same, you subtract the top numbers instead of adding them. Great question!

Okay, what was your question, Student D?

Teacher: No, not?  You’re good?  Okay.
Teacher: So what is the first step that we take when we’re going to add these mixed numbers with unlike denominators, Student T? What’s the first thing we should look at?
Student: Look at the fractions.
Teacher: Look at the fractions.  And we’ve got to make sure that the fractions have the same bottom number or we can’t add them. So what do we do, using math vocabulary, what do we do to make it possible to add, Student T?
Student: First look at the denominator, and then you look at both the numbers to see what number you can use to make them the same.
Teacher: What’s another word for that?  We’re making a…
Student: Equivalent.
Teacher: Equivalent fraction.  Yes, sir?
Student: Another one, I thought we’ve got to see if one can be a multiple of the other.
Teacher: I like that, that’s exactly right.  To save yourself some trouble you want to make sure you look at those.  For example number 2, we started with number 2, if you have a ¼ -- and we’re not looking at the whole numbers at this time – and a 1/2 – we wanted to see if they’re multiples of each other because that way we only have to change one of them. And in this case on number two, we did have.  2 is a multiple of 4.  So to make that an equivalent fraction, 1 x 2 is 2, 2 x 2 is 4, and then we can easily add those together.  You have ¼ plus 2/4 equals ¾.  Then we go over and we look at our whole numbers.  And so we have 1 and 2, and so 2 plus 1 is 3, so the answer would have been 3-3/4. We didn't have to do any regrouping with this one.  Any questions about that?  So the answer to 2 is 3-3/4.  The answer to 3 was 4-1/8.  The answer to 4 was 7-1/2.  The answer to 5 was 7-2/9.  6 was 12-1/6.  7 was 5-7/12.  And 8 was 4-3/5.  9 was 18-1/4, and 12 was 12-1/10.

Teacher: Okay.  So this morning, boys and girls, we are starting a whole new unit.  We finished on Friday unit one, and today we’re starting unit two which is going to carry us through all the way until about January.  The first skill that we’re going to talk about in unit one is multiplication.  Now we’re not going to start with our multiplication facts, because you should know your facts and we need to practice our facts.  And the standard that we’re going to be using and talking about is operations in algebraic thinking, 40A1, and it’s to determine multiplication equation as a comparison.  A lot of words there, and you’ll understand it more as we go.  We’ve always talked about everything we do relates to core standards, the common core that we are teaching in 4th grade, and that’s what you’re tested on, the standard.  This morning, on our screen, I’m going to put two questions.  Now it’s okay if you don’t understand it. What is it is I’m going to do a quick assessment to see how much of this you understand and how much need to do work.  Take your whiteboard this way here, the long way, and put a line down the middle, and your answer to question one is on the left side and your answer to question two is on the right side.  If you get stuck, don’t worry about it.  Just put what you think when you look at this question.  It’s question three and question four.  Take that back.  Question one and question three.  Sorry, guys.  Question one says if I know that 6 times 5 equals 30, what other multiplication problem do I know?  That’s what you’re going to put on the left hand side, and on the right hand side, it says represent the following multiplication problem in words.  They want you to write me a word problem.  Write me a word problem that represents that multiplication problem, okay? See what you can do.  Take about three minutes. Problem one goes on the left side.  Problem three goes on the right hand side.  Go ahead.  So as I’m walking around, I’m leaving you a ticket.  Just leave it on the corner of your desk, the corner I’m putting it on. If I didn’t get to you, just put your hand up.  Okay.  So let’s take a quick look at the first problem that you did.  So I have lots of green on the first problem, and green says you know what we’re looking for.  So the first questions asks, if I know that 6 times 5 equals 30, what other multiplication do I know?  What we’re looking for is another multiplication fact that represents the same thing as 6 times 5 equals. And the standard that we’re going to be using and talking about is operations in algebraic thinking, 40A1, and it’s to determine multiplication equation as a comparison.  A lot of words there, and you’ll understand it more as we go.  We’ve always talked about everything we do relates to core standards, the common core that we are teaching in 4th grade, and that’s what you’re tested on, the standard. Sorry, guys.  Question one says if I know that 6 times 5 equals 30, what other multiplication problem do I know?  That’s what you’re going to put on the left hand side, and on the right hand side, it says represent the following multiplication problem in words.  They want you to write me a word problem. Teacher: Okay.  One more time.  Student L, can you repeat it for us? Student J, can you repeat it for us?
Student: I have 4 bags of chocolates.  There is 3 chocolates in each bag.  How many do I have?

Teacher: Okay.  So this morning, boys and girls, we are starting a whole new unit.  We finished on Friday unit one, and today we’re starting unit two which is going to carry us through all the way until about January.  The first skill that we’re going to talk about in unit one is multiplication.  Now we’re not going to start with our multiplication facts, because you should know your facts and we need to practice our facts.  And the standard that we’re going to be using and talking about is operations in algebraic thinking, 40A1, and it’s to determine multiplication equation as a comparison.  A lot of words there, and you’ll understand it more as we go.  We’ve always talked about everything we do relates to core standards, the common core that we are teaching in 4th grade, and that’s what you’re tested on, the standard.  This morning, on our screen, I’m going to put two questions.  Now it’s okay if you don’t understand it. What is it is I’m going to do a quick assessment to see how much of this you understand and how much need to do work.  Take your whiteboard this way here, the long way, and put a line down the middle, and your answer to question one is on the left side and your answer to question two is on the right side.  If you get stuck, don’t worry about it.  Just put what you think when you look at this question.  It’s question three and question four.  Take that back.  Question one and question three.  Sorry, guys.  Question one says if I know that 6 times 5 equals 30, what other multiplication problem do I know?  That’s what you’re going to put on the left hand side, and on the right hand side, it says represent the following multiplication problem in words.  They want you to write me a word problem.  Write me a word problem that represents that multiplication problem, okay? See what you can do.  Take about three minutes. Problem one goes on the left side.  Problem three goes on the right hand side.  Go ahead.  So as I’m walking around, I’m leaving you a ticket.  Just leave it on the corner of your desk, the corner I’m putting it on.  Remember, I said the first question was going to go on the left side and the second question was going to go on the right side.  The second problem, they don’t want you to write 3 as t-h-r-e-e.  They want you to write me a word problem that represents that equation.  Okay.  You did that for one, but does that have anything to do with this one?  That’s what I want on the right hand side of your board, problem three, in words.  So erase that whole thing.  Write a word problem.  Okay.  Reread what you wrote there. One more minute.  You have enough room on this side? Okay.  I need all markers down.  Okay.  So most of you I’ve gotten to, and put in two different colors, or put two colors on your desk.  If I didn’t get to you, just put your hand up.  Okay.  So let’s take a quick look at the first problem that you did.  So I have lots of green on the first problem, and green says you know what we’re looking for.  So the first questions asks, if I know that 6 times 5 equals 30, what other multiplication do I know?  What we’re looking for is another multiplication fact that represents the same thing as 6 times 5 equals.  Now last week we touched upon really quick math triangles, and what were we using in the math triangles?  Student A?

Teacher: Okay, and what do we call them when we put multiplication and division together?
Student: Fact families?
Teacher: And what do we call it when we put addition and subtraction together?
Student: Fact families.

Teacher: You still left your parents.  Can you help me out, Student D? What’s the fact family for the—
Student: 6 times 5, 5 times 6.
Teacher: Nope.  Student A?
Student: 6 times 5 equals 30.  5 times 6 equals 30.
Teacher: Sure.  You’ve got to have your equals 30, don’t you?  You can’t just have 5 times 6?  Remember, our fact family has everybody included.  So up on the screen the fact family that they gave us was 6 times 5 equals 30, yes?
Multiple students: Yes.
Teacher: So on your boards put your board up if you have the opposite or the other – I shouldn’t say opposite, but the other fact family.  Up on the board they gave us 6 times 5 equals 30.  Put your board in front of you if you have the other one.  If you have a green card, that means you have the other one.  What does the other one say, Student E?

Student: 30 divided by 5 equals 6.  30 divided by 6 equals 5.

Student: It’s the largest number.
Teacher: And the sign you used on the sides going down?
Student: Division.
Teacher: And at the bottom?
Student: Multiplication.

Teacher: Nice job.  Excellent.  Good work, Student S.  Student A, can you stand up?  Student A, stand that way, because if you stand in front of it they can’t see you.  Can you tell us what Student S just said?  Why did he put 30 on the top?
Student: Because it’s larger.

Student: The largest number.
Teacher: Exactly.  You can’t divide and not have a negative number, or if you’re going to – if you start with a smaller number.  When we’re talking about multiplication and division, we always have to have the largest number at the top of the triangle, always.  It doesn’t change.  Give me a fact family starting with 5.

Teacher: Nice job.  Excellent.  Good girl.  Thank you.  So we’re going to definitely do a little more work with fact families, but is there anybody – Student M, why did you move your cards?  Is there anybody now that got a red or a yellow on the left side that doesn’t understand why?  You don’t have anything here.  We’re talking about the left side.  You have nothing there, right?  On the left side you got a green.  I’m not on this side yet.  I’m talking about the left side.  Is there anybody that got a red or a yellow on the left side?  You got one because you only copied the problem that was up there.  What should you have done?
Student: [Inaudible].
Teacher: Look at the fact family, honey.  They gave us 6 times 5 equals 30.  What is the other one you should have put on your board?  Put it there.  You understand?  Is there anyone else with a red or yellow on the left side of their desk that doesn’t understand why?  On the same aspect, because we’re going to talk – remember, come back here to the standard that we introduced this morning, and it says interpret – what does that word mean, interpret, when you interpret something?
Student: Switch it around.
Teacher: Okay.  Interpret doesn’t mean to switch, but I understand what you’re saying.  Go ahead, Student S.
Student: It’s a little more challenging.
Teacher: Believe it or not, interpret means that you understand.  To interpret something, you understand it.  You understand a multiplication equation.  What’s an equation?  Student A?
Student: It’s a problem with numbers that adds up to something.
Teacher: It would add up to something if we were doing an addition problem.  So we can’t use the words “adds up” right? Student J?
Student: It’s like—
Teacher: Not like.
Student: Numbers in a line.
Teacher: So if I just randomly put, that’s an equation?  Can you see it? You said numbers in a line.  Isn’t that what you said to me?  Aren’t those numbers in a line?  Your definition to me, Student J, was numbers in a line. Was numbers in a line an equation?  Think about it and I’ll come back.  Student S, what do you think an equation is?
Student: A group of numbers, like 5 plus 5 equals 10.
Teacher: You’re giving me an example.  I don’t want an example right now.  I want a definition.  Think about the example you gave me and how can we say that or write that as a definition for an equation?  So Student S says an example of an equation, 5 plus 5 equals 10.  What’s another example of an equation that we just did? Student H?
Student: 6 times 5 is 30.
Teacher: Student A said it was adding.  This one we added and this one we didn’t add, so we can’t say that it’s adding. Student C?
Student: It’s something that equals something.
Teacher: Okay, but randomly you can’t – I understand, you’re on the right track, but – she said it’s a couple of things that equal something, right?  So that doesn’t make sense.  I’m getting everybody going, “3 times 5 doesn’t equal 10.”  Student S, what do you think is an equation?
Student: An equation is a number sentence that has an answer.
Teacher: Nice job.  That’s true, right?  A number sentence that’s true, right, because I just gave you a number sentence that wasn’t true.  An equation would be a number sentence that the answer is correct or true, that makes sense, right?  So we want to interpret a multiplication equation as a comparison?  What does the word comparison mean?  I’m going to do what to it? Student J?
Student: Compare.
Teacher: Sure.  When you compare something, you look to see how they are—
Student: Different.
Teacher: We’re comparing them.  Contrasting them tells me if they’re different.  Comparison tells me if they’re—
Student: The same.
Teacher: The same or alike.  So we’re looking to see how they’re alike. Did we do that in problem number one?  6 times 5 equals 30, then 5 times 6 equals 30, right?  We compared them, yes? Do I have some yellows on the right hand side?  Those of you with yellow, you had started.  You had started and were in the right direction, and you might not have finished, or you didn’t put an answer.  You might have written a word problem for me, but you didn’t answer it.  You were right there.  Some of you were really close.  I’m going to ask the yellow people to share in a minute.  The people with red, you either didn’t answer or you weren’t understanding what we were looking for.  So as an example of a word problem using 3 times 4 equals 12, I could say to you I have 3 times as many candy bars as Student R has. Student R has 4 candy bars.  How many candy bars do I have? Student: 6 times 5 is 30.
Teacher: Student A said it was adding.  This one we added and this one we didn’t add, so we can’t say that it’s adding. Student C?
Student: It’s something that equals something.
Teacher: Okay, but randomly you can’t – I understand, you’re on the right track, but – she said it’s a couple of things that equal something, right?  So that doesn’t make sense.  I’m getting everybody going, “3 times 5 doesn’t equal 10.”  Student S, what do you think is an equation?
Student: An equation is a number sentence that has an answer.
Teacher: Nice job.  That’s true, right?  A number sentence that’s true, right, because I just gave you a number sentence that wasn’t true.  An equation would be a number sentence that the answer is correct or true, that makes sense, right?  So we want to interpret a multiplication equation as a comparison?  What does the word comparison mean?  I’m going to do what to it? Student J?
Student: Compare.
Teacher: Sure.  When you compare something, you look to see how they are—
Student: Different.
Teacher: We’re comparing them.  Contrasting them tells me if they’re different.  Comparison tells me if they’re—
Student: The same.
Teacher: The same or alike.  So we’re looking to see how they’re alike. Did we do that in problem number one?  6 times 5 equals 30, then 5 times 6 equals 30, right?  We compared them, yes?  Then I put up the second question, and the second question was much harder, and it’s meant to be harder.  It says represent the following multiplication problem in words.  So how many of you got a green on the right hand side of your desk?  Did I get anybody with a green on the right?  So I had nobody that totally understood it. Do I have some yellows on the right hand side?  Those of you with yellow, you had started.  You had started and were in the right direction, and you might not have finished, or you didn’t put an answer.  You might have written a word problem for me, but you didn’t answer it.  You were right there.  Some of you were really close.  I’m going to ask the yellow people to share in a minute.  The people with red, you either didn’t answer or you weren’t understanding what we were looking for.  So as an example of a word problem using 3 times 4 equals 12, I could say to you I have 3 times as many candy bars as Student R has. Student R has 4 candy bars.  How many candy bars do I have?  Student C?
Student: 13.
Teacher: Student C—
Student: 12.
Teacher: There you go.  Where did 13 come from?  How did you get 13? What were you doing in your head or on your hands that got you to 13?
Student: I took what Student R has and did it 3 times.
Teacher: And you got 13?  You did 3, 3 times?  3, 6, 9.
Student: And added 3 more.
Teacher: 12, yes?  So when you added 9 and 3, you got the wrong number maybe?  Okay.  So let’s – yellow people, if you have a yellow – Student A, want to stand up and share, read your problem first?
Student: [Inaudible].
Teacher: And you would complete that by saying—
Student: [Inaudible].
Teacher: Okay.  Somebody want to repeat Student A’s and don’t leave out the answer this time.
Student: I have three fifths candy – wait.  I have 4 bags of candy [inaudible].  3 chocolates.  [Inaudible].
Teacher: Is that what you said, Student A?
Student: No.
Teacher: Student A repeat it again, then somebody else repeat.
Student: I have 4 bags of chocolates.
Teacher: So what’s the first thing Student A told us?
Student: I have 4 bags of chocolate.
Teacher: She has 4 bags of chocolate.  Go ahead.
Student: [Inaudible].
Teacher: Slow down, Student A.  So 4 bags of chocolate.  What’s the second thing she tells me, Student A?
Student: There’s 3 in each one.
Teacher: 3 of what?
Student: 3 of chocolates.
Teacher: 3 of what, Student A?
Student: 3 chocolates.
Teacher: 3 chocolates in each—
Student: Bag.
Teacher: So 4 bags of chocolates, 3 chocolates in each bag.  What’s the question you’re asking, Student A?
Student: Put them together.
Teacher: How many what?  Do I?  Do I have, right?  So what’s the question she wants to know, Student A?
Student: How many chocolates does she have?
Teacher: Student C, can you repeat?
Student: How many – 4 bags of chocolate, 3 chocolates in each bag, how many chocolates does she have?
Teacher: Okay.  One more time.  Student L, can you repeat it for us? Student J, can you repeat it for us?
Student: I have 4 bags of chocolates.  There is 3 chocolates in each bag.  How many do I have?
Teacher: How many what?
Student: Chocolates.
Teacher: You can’t leave out the key words.  And the answer would be what, Student A?
Student: 12.
Teacher: 12 what, honey?
Student: 12 chocolates.
Teacher: In all.
Student: In each bag.
Teacher: Not in each bag.
Student: In all.
Teacher: Because how many do you have in each bag, honey?
Student: 3.
Teacher: Right.  You have 3 chocolates in each bag.  You can’t have 12 in each bag.  You have – the question was how many chocolates does she have, so she has 12 chocolates all together or total.  Who else has a yellow, and you are ready to bring it up?  Student S, why don’t you bring yours up, and we’re going to put in Student S’s missing pieces.  Put it on the screen.  We’re going to put in her missing pieces to turn her yellow to a red.  To a green, excuse me.  Can you read what you wrote?
Student: I had 4 triangles.  How many points will be in all?
Teacher: So who can repeat what Student S wrote?  Student M?
Student: If I had 4 triangles—
Teacher: Put your feet down.  One more time.  Can we repeat it on that side, Student D?
Student: If I had 4 triangles, how many points would I have?
Teacher: How can we change Student S’s wording just a little bit. Student S, can you try?  Look at what we did with Student A’s.
Student: 4 bags of chocolate.
Teacher: 3 chocolates in each bag.  How many chocolates does she have? How are we going to change the words for yours?  What did you start with?
Student: I have 4 triangles.
Teacher: Okay.
Student: How many points—
Teacher: You have to get the second piece of information, because you have to have both pieces of information, and think about your fact family.  What does Student S need to include in this, even though we all know—
Student: That she has 3 – that it—
Teacher: You’re sitting right in front of the camera.  Would you stop?
Student: That it multiplied to 3.
Teacher: No.
Student: That there are 3 points on each triangle.
Teacher: Even though you know there are 3 because it’s a triangle, you have to include your number 3, don’t you?  Okay.  So I have 4 triangles.  There are 3 – what did you call them?
Student: Points.

Student: Angles.
Teacher: But we’ll call them points just to go with your – we really want to call them angles though, right?  There are 3 points on each triangle, right?  The question will be—
Student: How many are in all?
Teacher: Your answer would be?
Student: 12.
Teacher: 12 what?
Student: Points all together.
Teacher: There are 12 points all together.  Yes?  Good girl.  Take yours away.  Who can repeat Student S’s?  Somebody I haven’t heard from yet.  Come on, Student D?
Student: Student S had 4 triangles.
Teacher: And there were—
Student: 3 points on each triangle.
Teacher: What did she want to know?
Student: How many points all together?
Teacher: And her answer was—
Student: There are 12 points all together.
Teacher: One more person with a word problem that was already on your board.  Student S?  Bring it up.  Okay.  Go ahead.
Student: I have 3 cookies.  I multiplied them by 4.  How many do I have?
Teacher: Okay.  Now you wouldn’t say you multiplied them by 4.  What kind of question would you ask?  You got a yellow because you started in the right direction.  You’re kicking me every time I go by there, love.  You’re going to start with, “I have 3 cookies.”  Okay.  What can we do 4 of that makes sense?  What could Student S do 4 of?
Student: There are 4 cookie trays.
Teacher: You have 3 cookies in 4 cookie trays? What would be the question?  What could Student S have 4 of?  Think here.  What would make sense?  Student H?
Student: You can have 4 pieces of cookies.
Teacher: If you have 3 cookies, you’d have 3 pieces of cookies?
Student: I have one.  I have 3 cookies.  I have 4 chocolate chips in each cookie.
Teacher: Nice.  There you go.  That’s perfect.  He’s going to have 4 chocolate chips on each cookie.  That would make sense, right?  So each cookie has – I’m going to abbreviate it 4 cc for chocolate chips.  The question would be—
Student: How many chocolate chips do I have in all?
Teacher: And your answer would be—
Student: 12 chocolate chips.
Teacher: Nice.  Nice work.  Okay.  Who can repeat Student S’s that makes sense?  Go ahead.
Student: Student S has 3 cookies.  Each one has 4 chocolate chips.  How many chocolate chips do I have?
Teacher: And his answer was—
Student: 12 chocolate chips.
Teacher: Nice.  So as you could see, when they asked us for a word problem to represent the numbers – that’s where we’re going to start our lesson today.  Right now I’m going to put up another problem.  Clean your boards completely.  And I want you to think for a minute.  I don’t want you to put anything on your board yet. How would you solve – how would you write this equation?  I don’t want an answer.  I want you to think for a second.  How would you write this equation?  Now when we don’t have an answer, we can use a letter to represent the missing number.  Now it doesn’t really matter what letter you use.  I always like the letter N, because N stands for number and you want it to be a number, right?  So for this morning I’m going to ask you to use the letter N for your missing number.  I don’t want you to solve it yet.  I want you to come up with an equation.  I want you to take two minutes and talk to your partner. Don’t write anything on your board right now, and decide between the two of you what equation could we be writing on the board that will make sense?  So let’s read out the question first.  Student A, can you read the question?
Student: Sammy has 4 bags of marbles.  Each bag has 12 marbles in it. Write an equation for the total number of marbles he has.  Solve it.
Teacher: So right now I don’t want you to solve the equation first. Right now I want you to think about how you would write out an equation for this problem.  Student S, I’m going to ask you to work with Student A and Student C.  Everyone has a partner besides that? Talk to your partner in your one inch voices as to how we can write out the equation.  Are you ready to share out?  Okay.  He’s going to tell you, but then you’ve got to tell him, right?  Come on, because we’re going to share in one minute.  Are you two ready to share out?
Student: Yes.
Teacher: What do you think you’re going to put?
Student: 4 times 3 equals 12.
Teacher: Do you agree with her, Student D?  Could you do it a different way?  You guys know what you’re going to do?
Student: Yes.
Teacher: What are you going to do?
Student: 4 times N equals 12.
Teacher: Could you do it a different way?
Student: 3 times N equals 12.
Teacher: I think you might want to look at your question one more time. Do you know what you’re going to write?
Student: Yes.  I can only write [inaudible].
Teacher: I want you to give me one first.
Student: 4 plus N equals 12.
Teacher: What would be the opposite?  What would be the other one to that?
Student: 3 plus N – no.  3 times 4 equals 12.
Teacher: Okay.  You two need to talk about it because you’re not on the same agreement right here.  You’re adding.  He’s multiplying.  That doesn’t work together.  What are you thinking?
Student: [Inaudible].
Teacher: What is yours going to be?
Student: [Inaudible].
Teacher: Can you help her?
Student: 12, 4 times.
Teacher: What kind of question is that?  I don’t want you to answer it. I want you to give me the full expression, honey.
Student: 12 times 4.
Teacher: You don’t need to give me an answer.  You guys ready
Student: Yeah.  4 times – 12 times 4 – [inaudible].
Teacher: You’re not giving me the expression either.  I need everyone to stop for a second.  I’ve walked around the room and I didn’t get to every group, but I have not heard the correct answer from any group yet.  So right now I’d like you to put what you thought was going to be the correct answer on your board and put it up in front of you.  Can you and your partner put the opposite problem?  If we’re doing fact families, whatever you have, your partner should have the other one. Student S, you should have one.  Your partner should have the other.  I didn’t ask for triangle.  I asked for an equation.  Sit down, Student J.  Remember what we were going to put.  What letter were we going to put for our missing answer because we didn’t want to answer it yet?
Student: N.
Teacher: N.  Okay.  Now do you guys have the same fact family going on there?  You’re supposed to.  That’s what you were chatting about.  You were supposed to come to an agreement.  You and your partner should have the opposite fact family on your board.  You were supposed to talk about it.  So he has a multiplication sign.  He has an addition sign.  Is that opposites?  No.  You have a 3 in your problem. He has a 4 in his problem.  Does the problem up there say anything about 3?  No.  Where should the N be?
Student: The answer.
Teacher: The N should be your answer.  Now what are the two parts of the equation we do know?  What two numbers do we know that they gave us in the story that they gave us?  We know that the missing number is the answer.  What are the two things we do know?  Student C?  What are the two things we do know, Student J?
Student: He has 4 bags of marbles.
Teacher: We know he has 4 bags of marbles, so what is our first number going to be?
Student: 4.
Teacher: So one of our numbers is going to be a 4.  What’s the second thing we know, Student J?
Student: There’s 12 marbles in each bag.
Teacher: What do we want to find out?  What was the question they asked us, Student R?  Just leave them.  What’s the question that they asked us?
Student: 4 times 12.
Teacher: No.  What is the question they asked us?
Student: [Inaudible].
Teacher: What do we want to find out, Student J?
Student: We want to find out the total amount of marbles we have.
Teacher: Sure, because we know he has 4 bags of marbles, and he has 12 in each, so what do we want to know?
Student: What is the total amount of marbles.
Teacher: How many marbles he has.  Marbles, 4 bags, right?  So if we have 4 bags of marbles – put the clips up here as our bags of marbles, and there are 12 marbles in each, can I add 4 and 12 and get my answer?
Multiple students: No.
Teacher: What do I have to do with the 4 and 12 to get my total number of marbles?  Head back to your seats, please.  What do I have to do, Student L?
Student: Multiply.
Teacher: And remember this morning when we said if we write the fact family this way, the other way to write it would be what?  So one of you should have this on your board, and the other person should have what, Student A?
Student: 12 times 4 equals N.
Teacher: Sure.  Okay.  Raise your hand if you and your partner both had one of these on your board.  Okay.  Hands down. Student T, how can Student J have his hand up and you don’t?  Aren’t you both partners?  What was the question I just asked?  Student J, can you tell him the question I just asked?
Student: If we both have the same thing, but different numbers in different places, raise your hand.

Teacher: Okay. Remember that? Then you cut it out and then you name the fractions also with that.  Still remember that?

Teacher: So today we’re going to use a 5 by 12 rectangle as a whole.  5 by 12 rectangles, 5 by 12 equals how many?
Multiple students: 60.
Teacher: 60 equal pieces, right?  I just wanted to start with a slide right there and ask you – everybody see the board?
Multiple students: Yes.
Teacher: So if I mix this rectangle into that situation, what could I do?  Yes?
Student: 30.
Teacher: Okay.  Can you come up and show us how you come up with 30?
Student: How?
Teacher: Mm-hmm.  Okay.  So for example, how could you divide this one into 30?  So this will be 5.  How many should be in the column situation?  The rows, there are 5 rows in the columns.  How many?
Student: 20.
Teacher: Louder.  What is it?
Student: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30.
Teacher: So I can make mine over here, right?  Thank you.  Go ahead.  So in this case this will become half.  Right?  When I look here what do you see as the whole?  This equals to what?  Half.  In this case half plus half equals a whole.  One whole.  Student S, do you understand in this case?  Now I’m moving them to another one so if I want to have the second 5 by 12 rectangles to turn into one fourth, how can I do that?  60 divided by 4. So it will be how many square units here?  15, right?  So one column of 5 will be 5, two columns, 10.  Three columns?
Multiple students: 15.
Teacher: Here will be one fourth.  Any questions so far?
Multiple students: No.
Teacher: How about if I move one?  Another one over here, what do you see?  In this combination, what do you see for the combination now?  You go to one half.  So that means this equals to the other side, right?  But can I write an equation that has the same thing like this? What is this?  Can you speak up a little bit?
Student: One fourth plus one fourth equals one half.
Teacher: Equals to—
Student: [Inaudible].
Teacher: Equals to one whole, right?  Some of you may have questions. Some of you may have questions.  If we don’t know this one, if we don’t know one fourth plus one fourth is equal to one half, you may think about how do you add it up to become a whole?  How?
Student: [Inaudible].
Teacher: A little bit—
Student: [Inaudible].
Teacher: Multiply which one?
Student: 4 times 4 and 4 times 4, and 1 times 4 to equal [Inaudible].
